coredata.dc:title: Extraction kinetics of cobalt and manganese with D2EHPA from lithium-ion battery recyclate
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Institution of Chemical Engineers.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Institution of Chemical Engineers.
coredata.dc:creator: Keller, A.
Sterner, P.L.
Hlawitschka, M.W.
Bart, H.-J.
coredata.dc:description: The separation of manganese from cobalt sulfate solutions is crucial in the field of spent lithium-ion battery recycling. A membrane-based microreactor is used to reveal required mass transfer characteristics as is diffusion and chemical reaction. The dependence of di-(2-ethylhexyl)phosphoric acid (D2EHPA) concentration, pH, and metal ion concentration on the extraction efficiency are investigated. A back-reaction term is introduced giving a significant influence on the effective extraction rate even at small metal complex concentrations as initial extraction rates proved to be inaccurate to describe the system. It is shown that the extraction of manganese at a pH of 3.0 is mainly diffusive while the cobalt extraction is strongly kinetically controlled. At a separating pH of 3.9 a good agreement between the model and experimental data is only given if the equilibrium back-extraction rate is considered (deviation less than 5%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solvent extraction
Chemical kinetics
Rate law
Back-reaction
Membrane-based micro-contactor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Slope analysis for estimation of the stoichiometric coefficient and equilibrium constant for cobalt and manganese (phase ratio: 3.96).
Log-molar flux versus log-metal ion concentration for 0.305 mol L−1 D2EHPA and a pH of 3.0.
Log-molar flux versus log-D2EHPA concentration for 0.01 mol L−1 initial metal concentration and pH of 3.0.
Log-molar flux versus log-hydrogen ion concentration for 0.01 mol L−1 initial metal concentration and 0.305 mol L−1 D2EHPA.
Relative reactive resistance for manganese and cobalt versus metal ion (A), D2EHPA (B), and hydrogen ion concentration (C), respectively (lines indicate trends).
Logarithmic reactive molar flux versus log-initial metal concentration (A), pH (B), and initial D2EHPA concentration (C).
Experimental and calculated extraction rate of manganese versus aqueous phase residence time at different pH; initial 0.01 mol L−1 Mn and 0.305 mol L−1 D2EHPA.
Extraction rate for cobalt and manganese for pH of 2.9 and initial feed concentrations of 0.01 mol L−1, each.
Extraction rate of manganese (A) and cobalt (B) for different preloading rates.

Calculation of the forward extraction rate constant
k
f , e
.

row.entry:
Mean value of
k
f , e

head.ce:title: Extraction kinetics of cobalt and manganese with D2EHPA from lithium-ion battery recyclate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chair of Separation Science and Technology, Technische Universität Kaiserslautern, Gottlieb-Daimler-Straße 44, 67653 Kaiserslautern, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chair of Separation Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chair of Separation Science and Technology, Technische Universität Kaiserslautern, Gottlieb-Daimler-Straße 44, 67653 Kaiserslautern, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Process Engineering, Johannes Kepler University, Altenberger Straße 69, 4040 Linz, Austria
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Process Engineering, Johannes Kepler University, Altenberger Straße 69, 4040 Linz, Austria

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The separation of manganese from cobalt sulfate solutions is crucial in the field of spent lithium-ion battery recycling. A membrane-based microreactor is used to reveal required mass transfer characteristics as is diffusion and chemical reaction. The dependence of di-(2-ethylhexyl)phosphoric acid (D2EHPA) concentration, pH, and metal ion concentration on the extraction efficiency are investigated. A back-reaction term is introduced giving a significant influence on the effective extraction rate even at small metal complex concentrations as initial extraction rates proved to be inaccurate to describe the system. It is shown that the extraction of manganese at a pH of 3.0 is mainly diffusive while the cobalt extraction is strongly kinetically controlled. At a separating pH of 3.9 a good agreement between the model and experimental data is only given if the equilibrium back-extraction rate is considered (deviation less than 5%).

ce:section.ce:para: The reduction of carbon dioxide emissions is crucial due to global warming. A shift of energy production to renewable sources and electrification of the transportation sector are intended to promote the achievement of the global climate goals. Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) show great potential for this purpose. They can be used for both grid storage and applications in electric vehicles, as they have a high-power density and energy efficiency. Especially the development and marketing of electric vehicles has increased drastically in the last years leading to high demand for battery cells (Harper et al., 2019). The global demand for batteries is assumed to double by 2025, correlating with an increasing number of electric vehicles and grid energy storage (Fan et al., 2020). The limited operating life of these batteries will inevitably result in future waste. Regarding the effort needed and environmental impact from the extraction of raw materials for battery production from primary sources, the recycling of end-of-life LIBs is a non-negotiable obligation and therefore subject to recent research (Granata et al., 2012). One of the most critical materials regarding the security of supply is the transition metal cobalt. While demand and price of this metal show a steady increase, a natural occurrence is concentrated in a few countries. Cobalt is used in LIBs as cathode material in a mixture of manganese, nickel, and carbon. While there are methods available for the digestion process of these compounds from LIBs mainly based on pyrometallurgy and subsequent acid leaching, the separation of these chemically similar elements is a challenge (Fan et al., 2020). Besides selective precipitation, the use of ionic liquids (Schaeffer et al., 2020), or electrochemical approaches (Lupi et al., 2005), solvent extraction using liquid ion exchangers is a promising technique to tackle this task. It is commonly used in hydrometallurgy, providing a continuous operation mode, high throughputs, and easily manageable equipment (Wellens et al., 2013; Bart, 2005). Recent studies target the extraction of cobalt (Wellens et al., 2012), especially from LIBs (Quintero-Almanza et al., 2019). The choice of the reactive extractant used to transfer metal from its ionic form to the organic phase is a crucial factor of extraction selectivity. It has already been shown that the liquid ion exchanger di-(2-ethylhexyl)phosphoric acid (D2EHPA) is especially suitable for the separation of cobalt and manganese (Hoh et al., 1984; Keller et al., 2021a).

Continuous operation of solvent extraction is carried out in mixer-settler cascades, or extraction columns. For apparatus design and process optimization residence time and kinetics are crucial. The mass transfer regime may be dominated by diffusion, reaction, or both. The diffusive mass transport can be expressed by well-known Sherwood correlations (van Male et al., 2004; Farahani et al., 2021; Zhang, 2005). The chemical reaction resistance strictly depends on the system and the concentrations, which have to be determined separately in each case. In the literature, the extraction kinetics for manganese and D2EHPA is well studied (Hughes and Biswas, 1993; Biswas et al., 1996; Biswas and Mondal, 2003). Biswas and Mondal (2003) carried out kinetic studies in a Lewis cell and a Hahn cell (non-agitated Lewis cell) reporting a mass transfer rate as to:
(1)
F =
10
- 6.69
M
n
2 +
H +
- 1
R H
2

The molar flux F is based on the species concentrations (manganese, hydrogen-ions, and dimeric D2EHPA) assuming a reaction mechanism to be as:
(2)
M n
H 2
O
n
2 +
⇌ M n
H 2
O
n - 1
2 +
(3)
M n
H 2
O
n - 1
2 +
+ R
R H
-
⇌ M n H
R 2
H 2
O
n - 1
+

Eq. (3) is expected as the rate-determining step, which occurs at the interface. However, the diffusion layer was still existing in the Lewis cell experiment, as higher agitation would disturb the planar interfacial area and a pure diffusion-free kinetic regime could not be reached. Even so, Eq. (1) represents the initial reaction rate being valid only in the very first minutes. For a profound analysis also the backward reaction term has to be considered, which can be easily derived from a stripping procedure (Willersinn, 2017).

In this study, a well-investigated micro-structured membrane extraction apparatus is used (Willersinn, 2017), where the interfacial area is not disturbed by stirring as in the Lewis cell. As the geometry and the hydrodynamic behavior are well-known, the diffusion resistances can be easily calculated even in a mixed kinetical regime. This study compares the extraction kinetics of manganese and cobalt, both extracted from aqueous sulfate solutions with D2EHPA dissolved in Isopar L®. The molar flux for different concentrations of metal, extractant, and hydrogen ions from the aqueous to the organic phase is investigated. The effect of the pre-loading of extractant is investigated in order to take the backward reaction into account. With the obtained reaction kinetics and diffusion kernels, a mathematical approach for numerical calculations of extraction rates is rated in its accuracy.

In a first step, an equilibrium study is carried out, as different metal complexes are mentioned in literature for manganese and cobalt, respectively (Grimm and Kolařík, 1974; Biswas et al., 2005; Tsai and Tsai, 2011). In the following, the overall mass transfer and diffusive influence are discussed based on data of the initial extraction rate, neglecting any backward reaction. The chemical resistance is derived and an empirical rate law is presented. Considering backward reaction, the law of mass action was conventionally used to calculate its kinetics. However, this is only valid for elementary reactions and cannot be applied in this case. Thus, in a more detailed analysis, the kinetics term is modified considering the backward reaction and its relevance on the modeling results are shown.

The overall chemical reaction of a divalent metal-ions with D2EHPA (cationic ion exchanger, R H ), can be formulated as
(4)
M
e
2 +
+ n
R H
2
¯
⇌
M e
R 2
R H
n - 1
¯
+ 2
H +
.

The bars indicate the presence in the organic phase, where D2EHPA is in its dimeric form in aliphatic solvents (Kolarik, 1982). The stoichiometric coefficient n depends on the metal ion and its concentrations in the organic phase (Boukraa, 2020). The law of mass action can be derived from this equilibrium reaction in its logarithmic form as
(5)
log 10

Here, the definition of the partition coefficient

is given as
(6)

n - 1

2 +
.

A broad range of possible metal complexes is described in the literature (Grimm and Kolařík, 1974; Biswas et al., 2005; Tsai and Tsai, 2011; Barnes et al., 1976). For manganese, these include
M n
R 2
¯
(Barnes et al., 1976),
M n
R 2
( R H )

(Keller et al., 2021b),

R 2

(Tsai and Tsai, 2011), and
M n
R 2

¯
(Biswas et al., 2005; Tsai and Tsai, 2011). Concerning cobalt the formation of
C o
R 2
¯
(Barnes et al., 1976),
C o
R 2

2
¯
(Grimm and Kolařík, 1974), and
C o
R 2
R H
3
¯
(Huang and Tsai, 1990) complexes are proposed.

Mass transfer experiments are carried out in a countercurrent mode. The overall mass transfer coefficient from the aqueous phase can be calculated as
(7)

F
.

Based on the two-film theory by Lewis and Whitman (1924), the required overall mass transfer coefficient is calculated with the resistance-in-series model (Gabelman and Hwang, 1999). Therein the reciprocal individual mass transfer resistances are added. Eq. (10) is suitable for a hydrophobic porous membrane fully wetted by the organic phase as used in this work.
(10)
1
K W
=
1
k
a q
+
1
k r
+
1
P
M e

+
1
P
M e
k
o r g

The diffusional resistances in the aqueous and organic phase (
k
a q
and
k
o r g
), the membrane pore resistance
k m
and the reaction resistance
k r
are all in series as the reaction takes place at the interface, as the solute is insoluble in the organic phase. All resistances occurring in the organic phase are additionally weighted by the distribution coefficient
P
M e
. The diffusion layer resistances are described by the Lèvéque equation (Gabelman and Hwang, 1999; Crowder and Cussler, 1998). A Sherwood correlation for rectangular microchannels is formulated (van Male et al., 2004) and successfully used in previous mass transfer studies (Willersinn, 2017; Willersinn and Bart, 2015):
(11)
S h =
k
a q
d h
D
= x
R e
y
S c
3
d h
L
z

It is dependent on the channel geometry given by the channel length L , the hydraulic diameter
d h
, and the molecular diffusion coefficient D . The turbulence in the channel is characterized by the Reynolds number R e . The quotient of momentum diffusivity ν and mass diffusivity D described by the Schmidt number S c
(12)
S c =
ν D
.

The parameters x ,
y ,
z are adaptable and depend on the channel geometry (Willersinn, 2017; Willersinn and Bart, 2015) in our case as:
(13)
S
h
a q
=
k
a q
d h

= 5.07

e 0.27

(14)

=

= 5.07

0.44

As a result of the identical geometry of both channels, the Sherwood correlations for the aqueous phase and organic phase appear in the same form. Mass transfer resistance km in the membrane can be derived from the membrane properties porosity ɛ , tortuosity τ and membrane thickness δ (Willersinn and Bart, 2016).
(15)
k m
=
D
o r g
ɛ
τ δ
For lack of complete membrane data, the tortuosity is estimated as the reciprocal porosity as described in (Willersinn, 2017).

The diffusion coefficients for the aqueous phase are obtained from literature (Buffle et al., 2007). The validity is given for a temperature of 298.15 K at infinite dilution. Effects of pH on the diffusion coefficients (Xiong et al., 1987) are neglected in this study. The diffusion coefficients of the metal-D2EHPA complex are estimated by using the Wilke-Chang equation (Wilke and Chang, 1955)
(16)
D
M e , o r g
= 7.4 ∙
10
- 8
M
T
ν
v m 0.6
.

The diffusion term is calculated based on temperature T , molar mass M and viscosity ν of the solvent and the molar volume
v m
of the solute. The latter is obtained by the LeBas method (Thorpe, 1915) as described in Tombs et al. (2014), as the sum of all individual atomic volumes in a molecule. The parameters for this group-contribution method are obtained from the literature (Tombs et al., 2014; Singman, 1984), while the viscosity of the solvent ( ν = 1.59
m
m 2
s
- 1
) and the molar mass of the solvent is assumed to equal n-dodecane.

Extraction kinetics are determined using the flux-method (F-method) (Hughes and Biswas, 1993, 1991). It is widely proved suitable for obtaining solvent extraction kinetics in different apparatuses and has already been used for a membrane micro-contactor (Willersinn and Bart, 2016). The mass transfer can be expressed as
(17)
-
d
M
e
2 +
a q
d t
=
d
M
e
2 +
o r g
d t
=
k
f , e
A V
M
e
2 +
a 1
R H
a 2
H +
a 3
.

The mass transfer is dependent on the concentration of the metal ion
M
e
2 +
in the aqueous phase, the concentration of the extractant D2EHPA
R H
and the aqueous concentration of hydrogen ions
H +
to the power of the extraction orders
a 1
…
a 3
. The apparatus channel volume is V . The overall extraction rate is given by the forward extraction rate constant
k
f , e
. Normalized with the surface area A , the molar flux F for is obtained as:
(18)
F e
=
k
f , e
M
e
2 +
a 1
R H
a 2
H +
a 3

This molar flux strongly depends on the apparatus geometry A/V thus the geometry free reaction kinetics is indicated by the subscript r. The molar flux
F r
solely resulting from the chemical reaction is given by:
(19)
F r
=
k
f , r
M

2 +

b 3
with the reaction rate constant

f , r
and the reaction order
b 1
…

. In sum, the overall mass transfer coefficient can be expressed as follows:
(20)
1
K W
=
1

+
Δ

L M

P

+

P
k
o r g

When experimentally deriving KW and subtracting all diffusional resistances the chemical reaction term will remain. The relative reactive resistance
k
r , r e l
is used as an indicator for kinetically limited reaction. It is defined as the quotient of the reaction resistance and the sum of all individual resistances:
(21)
k
r , r e l
=
K W
k r

For kinetic experiments, an automated extraction setup is used. It consists of a micro-contactor, two pumps for the organic and aqueous phase, and an autosampler. The schematic is depicted in Fig. 1 .

The key component is a flat sheet membrane module, described in Willersinn and Bart (2016). In this study, mill-cut PTFE spacers are used, resulting in microchannels with a rectangular cross-section of 3 mm width, 0.5 mm height, and 405 mm length. The channels for the organic phase and aqueous phase are identical. The separation of the two phases is realized by using a hydrophobic PTFE membrane (Omnipore JHWP14225, Merck Millipore) with a thickness of δ = 65
μ m , a porosity of ɛ = 0.8 , and an estimated tortuosity τ = 1.28 (Willersinn, 2017; Armour and Cannon, 1968).

For the organic phase, a peristaltic pump (120U DM/2, Watson-Marlow) is connected to the contactor. The membrane pores need to be filled with the organic phase in the beginning. Therefore, the organic outlet is blocked till the aqueous phase channel is filled with the organic phase. Afterward, the organic phase is replaced by purging the aqueous phase through its channel. The aqueous feed is supplied by a self-made syringe pump. The flow rate for the aqueous and organic phase can be varied from 2.03 ×
10
- 8
down to 1.96 ×
10
- 9
m 3
s
- 1
and 6.08 ×
10
- 8
to 3.92 ×
10
- 9
m 3
s
- 1
, respectively. This enables residence times in the range of 30–310 s for the aqueous phase. The organic flow rate is in each experiment two times higher than that of the aqueous phase. This ensures that the organic phase concentrations remain constant over the length of the reactor. The collection of raffinate samples takes place with an autosampler, which provides space for six samples. After each change of the flow rate, the extraction process has to become steady state. Therefore, three times the aqueous channel volume of raffinate is discarded before collecting the next sample. According to Eqs. 17 and 18, the molar flux is calculated by the raffinate concentration over time. The standard deviation of the resulting slope is given in Figs. 3 to 5 and 7.

The extraction kinetics are generally dependent on the aqueous metal concentration, the organic D2EHPA concentration, and the concentration of hydrogen ions. Therefore, for each metal three sets of experiments are carried out, where two of the three concentrations are kept constant, while one is varied. Constant hydrogen ion concentration is achieved when using an aqueous buffer solution. In a second step, the influence of pre-loaded D2EHPA is investigated. The concentration ranges of the aqueous and organic phases for each experiment are given in Table 1 .

The aqueous phase is prepared with de-ionized water (≤0.01 µS/cm). Cobalt sulfate heptahydrate (purity: >97.4%, CAS: 10026-24-1) and manganese sulfate monohydrate (purity: 99–101%, CAS: 10034-96-5) are purchased from Carl Roth and Honeywell Fluka, respectively. Buffer solutions are prepared of monosodium phosphate monohydrate (purity: 99%, CAS: 10049-21-5) and ortho-phosphoric acid (purity: 85%, CAS: 7664-38-2). The ionic strength of the buffer is adjusted to 0.2 mol L−1. Sulfuric acid (Bernd Kraft, purity: 95–97%, CAS: 7664-93-9) is used for the regeneration of the extractant. All reagents are of analytical grade. The feed solution, as well as the raffinate concentrations, are determined by atom emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, Spectro Genesis). Based on the data of the manufacturer a systematic error of 2% is assumed, which is shown in Figs. 8 to 10. Moreover, all experiments are performed in duplicate. A standard deviation of the raffinate concentration of 3% is observed between the two trials. For pH measurements, a pH-meter (inoLab Level 2, WTW) and Sentix 81 (Xylem Analytics) probe are used.

D2EHPA is supplied by Bayer and used without further purification. A purity of 82% (w/w) is titrimetrically analyzed according to Ramachandra Rao and Acharya (1993). The extractant is diluted in Isopar L® from Exxon Mobile Corp. (CAS: 64742-48-9). It is a kerosene-like aliphatic solvent mainly consisting of C11–C13 alkanes. D2EHPA solution is regenerated before each experiment by stirring in an equal volume of 1 mol L−1 sulfuric acid. Subsequent centrifugation for 10 min at 3500 rpm (Biofuge Primo R, Thermo Scientific) and decanting removes traces of the aqueous phase. For the preparation of pre-loaded D2EHPA, an excess of 1 mol L−1 metal sulfate solution is equilibrated with the organic phase. Diluted sodium hydroxide solution (prepared of sodium hydroxide pellets, Sigma-Aldrich, purity: >98.0% CAS: 1310-73-2) is added in drops to keep the pH constant at 3. Equilibrium is assumed when no changes in pH occur over one hour. Organic metal concentration is determined by stripping a defined volume of the solution with three times the volume of 1 mol L−1 sulfuric acid. The metal concentration in the aqueous phase is then analyzed and the corresponding organic concentration is calculated by mass balance. Different pre-loadings can be obtained by diluting with an un-loaded D2EHPA solution.
The partition coefficient is obtained by shaking experiments and the results for manganese and cobalt are shown in Fig. 2 .

For varied concentrations of the D2EHPA dimer (0.019 to 0.153 mol L−1) the logarithm of the partition coefficient −2 pH lies in a range from −9 to −6.5 and −7 to −4 for cobalt and manganese, respectively. By analysis of the slope and intercepts, the stoichiometry of the complex formation can be derived. Eq. (4) can be reformulated with n
=
1.5 for Co and Mn:
(22)
M
e
2 +
+ 1.5
R H
2
¯
⇌
M e
R 2
R H
¯
+ 2
H +
,
M e : C o ,
M n

With this the partition coefficient dependent on pH and the concentration of D2EHPA dimer is as:
(23)
log 10
⁡
P
M n
= 3.14
log 10
⁡
R H
2
¯
+ 2 p H - 1.58
(24)
log 10
⁡
P
C o
= 2.87
log 10
⁡
R H
2
¯
+ 2 p H - 3.94

The kinetics of the extraction of manganese and cobalt are investigated by applying the “F”-method, revealing the initial forward extraction rate constant. The backward reaction of the complex formation reaction is therefore neglected. This assumption for the initial reaction rate is valid in the very first minutes and for a low concentration of extracted solute in the organic phase, which is ensured during the experiments by using excess amounts of solvent. According to Eq. 18, the influencing factors on molar flux are the aqueous metal ion concentration
[
M e
2 +
] , the hydrogen ion concentration [
H +
] and the concentration of ion exchanger in the organic phase [ R H ] , which are all individually investigated. The results from these experiments are depicted in log-log plots in Figs. 3 to 5
covering the varied concentrations.

In Fig. 3 the aqueous metal feed concentration of manganese is varied from 2.5 to 100 mmol L−1, while extractant concentration and pH are kept constant at 0.305 mol L−1 D2EHPA and a pH of 3.0. The logarithmic molar flux in this range inclines from −8.6 to −7.6 with a slope of 0.65 ± 0.05. For cobalt, the investigated concentration range of 5–50 mmol L−1 results in a logarithmic molar flux from −9.2 to −8.2. With 0.97 ± 0.19, the slope is steeper for cobalt than for manganese. The y-axis intercepts are 6.81 for manganese and 6.85 for cobalt. The analysis for cobalt entails higher relative insecurity due to the overall low extract concentration of cobalt. Subsequently, the influence of the extractant concentration is investigated. While the aqueous feed properties are kept constant with a metal concentration of 0.01 mol L−1 and a value of pH of 3.0, the organic concentration of D2EHPA is varied. The results are shown in Fig. 4.

For a varied concentration of D2EHPA from 0.019 to 0.305 mol L−1, the logarithmic molar fluxes lie in a range from −9 to −8.1 for manganese and −9.4 to −8.8 for cobalt, resulting in a slope of 0.83 ± 0.05 and 0.67 ± 0.06, respectively. The y-axis intercepts are −7.5 for manganese and −8.3 for cobalt. The influence of the pH is investigated with varied concentrations of hydrogen ions. Results are depicted in Fig. 5.

For manganese, the value of pH is varied in the range of 1.4 to 3.0. The logarithmic molar flux is therefore −8.8 to −8.0 with a slope of −0.60 ± 0.08 and a y-axis intercept of 9.78. The investigation of cobalt is carried out in a pH range of 2.5 to 3.5. The logarithmic molar fluxes lie in the range from −10.1 to −8.4 with a slope of −1.57 ± 0.33 and a y-axis intercept of −13.75. The slope of a linear fit of mass flow concerning the aqueous phase residence time is used to determine the molar fluxes according to Eq. 18. As it can be seen from Figs. 3 to 5, all data points fall on straight lines in good approximation. The slopes of the regression lines represent the reaction orders (extraction orders
a 1
,
a 2
,
a 3
) in the exponent of the concentration of the respective species. Inserting the ordinate intercepts in Eq. 18 and comparing the left and right-side result in values for
k
f , e
which are given in Table 2 .

The overall mass flux for the reactive extraction of manganese and cobalt from the aqueous phase to the organic phase containing D2EHPA is given by the following equations:
(25)
F
e , M n
= 8.73 ×
10
- 9
M n
2 +
0.65
R H
0.83
H +
- 0.60
(26)
F
e , C o
= 5.73 ×
10
- 12
C o
2 +
0.97
R H
0.67
H +
- 1.57

The maximum observed extraction yield is 28% of cobalt, while the extraction of manganese is reaching up to 64%. For most experiments, the extraction yield of cobalt does not exceed 10%. This is represented by the forward extraction rate
k
f , e
of manganese, which is about three orders of magnitude higher than for cobalt. The extraction orders of the metal and extractant concentration are comparable for both metals. Differences in the interpretation of the data can be explained by the statistic error of the slopes. The absolute value of the inhibiting extraction order of the hydrogen ion concentration is distinctly larger for cobalt with −1.6 compared to −0.6 for manganese. Even when the statistic error of 0.33 for the exponent of the hydrogen ion concentration is considered in the extraction kinetics of cobalt, the exponent is still two times larger than for manganese, which leads to a noticeably higher influence of the pH value on the extraction of cobalt.

The slow extraction rate of cobalt results in difficulties in obtaining reliable kinetic data. At low pH values and low D2EHPA concentrations, barely any metal is extracted and small analytical errors can lead to major uncertainties resulting in an inferior data situation compared to manganese.

The overall mass transfer during reactive extraction is the result of reactive and diffusive effects, where the latter is highly dependent on apparatus geometry and flow regime. The diffusive resistances are calculated according to Eqs. 13 and 14 and a subtraction from the overall flux yields. The chemical one is considered as a function of metal ion, D2EHPA, and H+ concentration (see Fig. 6 ). In a first step, the diffusive resistance in the microreactor is studied. The underlying diffusion data can be found in the supplementary data. The extraction of manganese is highly influenced by the diffusional resistance as can be seen in Fig. 6A. At p H
=
3.0 and [ R H ]
=
0.305
m o l
L
- 1
the mass transfer shifts from the diffusion to the kinetic regime with increasing manganese concentration, indicated by the dominating reactive resistance when approaching the plateau. Above [ M
n
2 +
]
=
0.075
m o l
L
- 1
, the relative reactive resistance (see Eq. 21) reaches a plateau at 71%. In Fig. 6B, a linear diminution of the relative reactive resistance can be seen with respect to the D2EHPA concentration at p H
=
3 and [ M n ]
=
0.01
m o l
L
- 1
. In terms of pH (see Fig. 6C) the relative reactive resistance increases from 28 to 79% at a
H +
concentration of 0.001 mol L−1 to 0.015 mol L−1
( [ R H ]
=
0.305
m o l
L
- 1
, [ M n ]
=
0.01
m o l
L
- 1
). Similar results are obtained with cobalt, but this system is generally more kinetically limited than the manganese one. An increased D2EHPA concentration leads to a stronger diffusive influence. An elevated hydrogen ion concentration results in a kinetically limited mass transfer. In contrast to manganese, the diffusive resistance increases with an increasing cobalt metal ion concentration.

The extraction rates of cobalt are significantly lower than of manganese (see Fig. 7 ). The diffusion properties of the two metals are quite similar (
D
M n , a q
= 7.12
×
10
- 10
,
D
C o , a q
= 7.32 ×
10
- 10
), making the reaction rate the only reason for this behavior. Diffusive resistances can mostly be neglected in the extraction system Co/D2EHPA. The proportion of reaction in the overall mass transfer is higher than 60% for every point of operation (see Fig. 6). Similar to manganese, very low extraction rates occur at low pH and low concentrations of D2EHPA.

The reaction kinetics term based on Eq. (19) are displayed in Fig. 7 as log-log plots of molar fluxes
F r
concerning the varied concentration [ M
e
2 +
] , [ R H ] , and [
H +
] .

The logarithmic reactive molar flux increases linearly with the pH value (Fig. 7A: increasing H+ concentration). An enhanced molar flux can also be seen with rising metal ion concentrations (see Fig. 7B). For logarithmic D2EHPA concentrations (see Fig. 7C) smaller than −1.5, data points for manganese have to be ignored because they result in negative reaction rates and are therefore implausible. Data for cobalt at extractant concentrations lower than 0.076 mol L−1 fall on a straight line likewise data for the molar flux (see Figs. 4 and 7C). Data for manganese can only be investigated in a range of pH from 1.8 to 2.8, while for cobalt pH values lower than 2.8 cannot be displayed in the plot because deviations in the diffusion models are larger than the observed extraction rate. The obtained reaction kinetics can be expressed by:
(27)
F
r , M n
= 2.63 ×
10
- 9
M n
2 +
0.47
R H
0.81
H +
- 0.63
(28)
F
r , C o
= 7.07 ×
10
- 11
C o
2 +
0.93
R H
0.63
H +
- 1.23
.

The underlying data for these kinetic equations are collected in a pH range of 1.4–3.0, and 2.8–3.6 for manganese, and cobalt, respectively. Extrapolation to a broader range with sufficient accuracy will be shown later. It is notable, that the slope of all logarithmic molar fluxes and therefore the exponents in the reaction kinetic are roughly double the value of cobalt than for manganese. Even if the relative high statistic errors of the linear regression of up to 35% are considered, this difference cannot be explained by uncertainties. While the reaction orders of the complex formation of cobalt, and manganese with D2EHPA are assumed to be similar in the equilibrium state, the empirical kinetic approaches differ for both metals.

A comparison of the calculated and experimental obtained data is shown in Fig. 8 . Here, the normalized manganese concentration (referring to the feed) is plotted against the residence time in the micro-contactor. It shows that the qualitative trend is well represented for the pH values 1.4, 2.1, and 3.0. However, increasing deviations are given with higher pH values. This is associated with progressive diffusion limitation. Therefore, it is assumed that the diffusion model overestimates slightly the diffusive resistance as the deviations are within 10% error. As a consequence, the extraction yield is underestimated.

Fig. 9 shows the modeling results at a pH of 2.9 for an equimolar mixture of manganese and cobalt. The extraction rate of cobalt is slightly overestimated by the model, while manganese is extracted more effectively than the model predicts. Cobalt extraction yield does not exceed 10% during investigated extraction time, while over 60% of manganese could be transferred to the organic phase. The extraction selectivity is in favor of manganese, which is consistent with earlier studies (Hoh et al., 1984).

The backward reaction (see Eq. 4) of the metal complexes in the organic phase (preloaded D2EHPA) results in free D2EHPA and metal ions. This affects the extraction rate and the existing model for the initial extraction rate is extended by a back extraction term:
(29)
F
r , l
=
k
f , r
M
e
2 +
b 1
R H
b 2
H +
b 3
-
k
f , l
M e
R 2
R H
m
with the back extraction constant
k
f , l
and m the order of the backward reaction. As the pre-loading influences the extraction strongly, only metal complex concentrations of lower than 28.8 mmol L−1 and 2.3 mmol L−1 for manganese and cobalt, respectively, were investigated. The effects on the extraction rate of manganese are shown in Fig. 10 A. After 300 s, the extraction yield lowers to 60% resp. 20% starting from a preloading of 0.0048 mol L−1 to 0.0288 mol L−1. Already at a preloading of 0.019 mol L−1, the extraction gets mainly kinetically hindered (see supplementary data). It is to mention, that the model is only valid for pH lower than 3.0. The extrapolation to the investigated pH of 3.95 seems to be suitable in this case. This data is not directly comparable with the data gained from kinetic studies of manganese, because of the relatively high pH of 3.96, which would normally result in the extraction taking place in a dominating diffusive regime. This underlines the inhibiting effect of an increased organic metal concentration.

Cobalt is even stronger affected by a raised initial loading in the organic phase. The backward reaction is favored by the low pH, resulting in a very low overall extraction rate. The results of these experiments are shown in Fig. 10B. The plot depicts trends of the extraction rate in a range from 12% (Fig. 10B: 88%) down to 5% (Fig. 10B: 95%) for pre-loading rates of 1%–10% of the maximum equilibrium loading rate. The absolute value of the initial organic metal concentration is ten times lower than for manganese with a maximum of 0.0023 mol L−1. For higher pre-loading rates, no extraction at all can be measured with the analytic methods used.

Subsequently, the following empirical kinetic equations are formulated:
(30)
M n :
F
r , l
= 2.63 ×
10
- 9
M n
2 +
0.47
R H
0.31
H +
- 0.63
- 8.95 ×
10
- 8
M n
R 2
R H
0.11
(31)
C o :
F
r , l
= 7.07 ×
10
- 11
C o
2 +
0.93
R H
0.63
H +
- 1.23
- 8.33 ×
10
- 5
C o
R 2
R H
1.77

The backward reaction constant is one order of magnitude larger than the forward reaction constant for manganese and even six orders of magnitude larger for cobalt. With a higher organic metal concentration, cobalt extraction declines exponentially, while the effect of manganese is logarithmic and therefore less significant. This leads to the conclusion, that the back extraction of manganese has only a small influence on the overall mass transfer while it has to be considered for cobalt. The difference between modeled and measured data does not exceed 5% and is mostly within the insecurities of the measurement. Although the evaluated equations are only valid for a limited range of conditions, they can be beneficial for further developments of reactive extraction models.

The reactive extraction of manganese and cobalt from aqueous sulfate solutions with D2EHPA is investigated using a nondispersive, micro-structured membrane contactor. This apparatus is characterized by a fixed interfacial area regardless of the flow regime. The well-known geometry and hydrodynamic behavior enable the calculation of chemical kinetics even in a mixed kinetical regime. For the cobalt-D2EHPA system, it is shown, that reaction kinetics is highly dependent on pH, the concentration of extractant, and initial organic metal concentration. The obtained reaction orders are 0.93, 0.63, and −1.23 for cobalt, monomeric D2EHPA, and hydrogen ion concentration, respectively. Compared to manganese, the reaction rate is two orders of magnitude lower. Consequently, cobalt in contrast to manganese extraction is dominated by a kinetic regime. The reaction orders of manganese extraction differ markedly from that of cobalt and read as 0.47, 0.31, and −0.63 for manganese, monomeric D2EHPA, and hydrogen ion concentration, respectively.

The developed models depict the extraction rate data satisfyingly. For manganese concentrations of 0.005 to 0.1 mol L−1, D2EHPA concentrations of 0.0038 to 0.305 mol L−1, and pH between 1.4 and 3.0, the calculated extraction rate deviates less than 5% from measured values. For cobalt, the model is validated in concentration ranges of 0.005 to 0.05 mol L−1 cobalt, 0.076 to 0.305 mol L−1 D2EHPA, and a pH of 2.8 to 3.2. The absolute deviation between calculated and measured values is comparable to that of manganese.

Commonly in literature, the backward reaction is mainly neglected. In this study, it was shown that the presence of organic metal complexes has a significant influence on mass transfer even at low concentrations. The extraction rate decreases rapidly in the presence of metal complexes. Using a backward reaction term, the overall mass transfer from the aqueous to the organic phase could be modeled within a deviation of 5% in the case of a superimposed reaction.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

All foci coordinates, activation probability maps, in addition to the supplemental information will be available on ANIMA: a data-sharing initiative for neuro-imaging meta-analyses:anima.fz-juelich.de.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cherd.2022.01.005.
